steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student wants to refute the claim that slow TV is boring, using a quotation. The correct choice must (1) push back on the "boring" label and (2) include a direct quote that supports the refutation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "*MORA: Zeichner* can afford a ‘visceral kind of armchair tourism,’ as Heller puts it.": Uses the quote but never challenges the "boring" claim — it just describes the show. ✗
  • Choice B — "While some have praised slow TV for affording ‘a visceral kind of armchair tourism,’ others have called it ‘the world's most boring television.’": Presents both views without taking a side; this is balanced, not a refutation. ✗
  • Choice C — "Far from boring, slow TV programs can provide a ‘visceral kind of armchair tourism,’ as Heller puts it, whereby viewers can watch artists at work in real time.": "Far from boring" is a direct refutation, and Heller's quote is the supporting evidence. Both requirements are met. ✓
  • Choice D — "With broadcasts of ordinary events, like artists at work, occurring in real time, slow TV just might be ‘the world's most boring television.’": Endorses the "boring" claim instead of refuting it. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
